_about: Word Histories — flagship long-form pages on a term's origin, etymology, and historical usage. Grounded in Ted's databases + PDF library via scripts/research.py (see docs/RESEARCH_METHOD.md). Each 'sources' entry cites a real work surfaced in data/research/<slug>.json — verify against the source before quoting. Rendered in the 'Word History' tab + histories.html. (File name kept as essays.json for pipeline stability.)

wizard.title: Wizard: the wise man who went wrong
wizard.dek: The word began as a compliment. Watch a sage curdle into a suspect.
wizard.html:
Magic: The Gathering has 1,288 creatures with the Wizard type — its single largest class of magic-user, and, tellingly, its bluest, blue being the game's color of knowledge. That is exactly right, because wizard began not as a word for power but as a word for wisdom. Middle English wysard, first recorded around 1440, joined wys ('wise') to the suffix -ard, and it meant a philosopher, a sage — a wise man, full stop.

Then it curdled. The suffix -ard is a souring one: it makes drunkard, coward, sluggard, dullard, and by the 1550s it was doing the same to the wise man. A wizard came to mean one whose wisdom had strayed into forbidden art — a knower of too much. The hardening tracks a real historical squeeze: this is the century in which England began prosecuting its 'cunning folk,' the village healers and finders whose learning, as Keith Thomas shows in Religion and the Decline of Magic, was recategorized from useful to illicit. The word's fall is the culture's suspicion, fossilized.

Fantasy then restored the honorific. Tolkien's Gandalf (1954) is a sage before he is a spellcaster, and the game inherits that dignity: its wizards study, and in Strixhaven (2021) the whole setting became a university of them. But both lives of the word survive on the cards — the revered adept and the meddler who knew too much. A wizard is, etymologically, only a wise guy; whether that is a compliment has always depended on who is saying it.

sources.label: Etymonline, s.vv. wizard, -ard
Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (cunning folk)
the corpus (Wizard creature type, color distribution)

sorcerer.title: Sorcerer: the dice buried in the word
sorcerer.dek: Hidden inside 'sorcery' is an ancient technique — the casting of lots.
sorcerer.html:
A sorcerer and a wizard are, on the surface, the same thing — a worker of magic — but the words carry different weather. The wizard studies; the sorcerer channels, and keeps a whiff of the illicit the wizard has mostly shed. That difference is buried in the etymology, and it is a genuine surprise.

Sorcerer comes through Old French sorcier from Vulgar Latin sortiarius — literally 'a caster of lots,' from Latin sors, sortem, 'lot, fate.' A sorcerer was, at the root, not a spell-slinger but a diviner: one who told fortunes and manipulated fate by casting lots, drawing straws, opening a book at random. The same sors gives us sortilege, which the RenaissanceMagicDB defines precisely as 'divination through the casting of lots — dice, marked objects, or randomly selected passages.' The magical sense we now hear arrives in English only around the 1520s, layered over that older fortune-telling core.

Magic: The Gathering has, without meaning to, buried the word even deeper. In the game Sorcery is a card type — a spell you can cast only at 'sorcery speed,' on your own turn — a purely mechanical term with no fortune-telling left in it at all. And yet the pun holds: every Sorcery is drawn from the top of a shuffled deck, a throw of the dice. The word came full circle to chance without anyone noticing.

sources.label: Etymonline, s.v. sorcerer (Latin sortiarius ‹ sors)
RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. sortilegium
the corpus (Sorcery card type)

witch.title: Witch: the word older than the trials
witch.dek: One of the oldest magic-words in English, and among the most fought-over.
witch.html:
Witch is one of the oldest words in this whole lexicon, and one of the murkiest. Old English had wicce (feminine) and wicca (masculine), beside the verb wiccian, 'to work magic' — but the root beneath them is genuinely uncertain, perhaps tied to a sense of 'bending' or of 'sacred rite.' It is older than nearly every learned magic-word English later borrowed from Greek and Latin, and its pre-history is a real dead end. That obscurity is fitting for a word that has spent a thousand years being defined by other people.

Its darkest definition came in the early modern centuries. The witch trials killed tens of thousands, powered by manuals like the Malleus Maleficarum (1487) that fused folk maleficium, heresy, and a lurid demonology into a single crime. The great achievement of modern scholarship was to take that story apart: Norman Cohn's Europe's Inner Demons showed the sabbat to be a fantasy assembled by inquisitors from older libels, Keith Thomas set witchcraft in its social world, and Carlo Ginzburg's The Night Battles uncovered, beneath the trial records, a folk-magic reality — the Friulian benandanti who believed they fought witches in dream-battles for the harvest — that the courts could only hear as devil-worship.

Then the word was reclaimed. In 1954 Gerald Gardner's Wicca — a new religion stitched, as Ronald Hutton's The Triumph of the Moon demonstrates, from Romantic, Masonic, and folkloric threads, and from Margaret Murray's since-discredited witch-cult thesis — turned 'witch' into a chosen, positive identity. Magic: The Gathering inherits chiefly the fairy-tale witch of Eldraine and the covens of Innistrad. But the word holds all of it at once: the obscure ancient practitioner, the innocent burned as one, and the modern who claims the name with pride.

sources.label: Bosworth–Toller, s.vv. wicce, wicca
Norman Cohn, Europe's Inner Demons
Carlo Ginzburg, The Night Battles
Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon
Etymonline, s.v. witch

warlock.title: Warlock: the broken oath
warlock.dek: Not a male witch by origin — an oath-breaker. The pact is in the word.
warlock.html:
Modern fantasy treats a warlock as a male magic-user, often one who draws power from a bargain with a darker patron — the pact-caster of Dungeons & Dragons and of Magic: The Gathering. By origin the word has nothing to do with being male, and everything to do with the bargain.

Old English wǣrloga means 'oath-breaker, traitor' — wǣr, 'a pledge, a covenant,' plus -loga, 'a liar,' from lēogan, 'to lie.' A warlock is, at the root, a covenant-breaker, and the word's first great referent was the Devil himself: the archetypal traitor, the being who broke faith with God. Only later, and chiefly in Scots, did it drift toward 'a man in league with the Devil,' and thence to the fantasy 'male witch' — a folk-symmetry with witch that the etymology never actually supported.

What is uncanny is how the modern game-warlock circles back to the oath. The Dungeons & Dragons warlock is defined by a pact sworn to a patron — a fiend, a fey, a Great Old One — and the whole drama of the class is that the power is borrowed on terms. That is, precisely, a covenant, and the danger in it is precisely that covenants can be broken. A thousand years on, the warlock is still defined by an oath: not the pledge kept, like the paladin's, but the pledge made in the dark, and never quite safe.

sources.label: Bosworth–Toller, s.v. wǣrloga
Etymonline, s.v. warlock

curse.title: Curse: a word whose own origins are cursed
curse.dek: An etymological dead end — fitting for a word about doom.
curse.html:
Almost every word in this study can be traced somewhere — to Greek, to Latin, to Arabic, to a Persian priesthood. Curse cannot. Old English curs appears before 1050 with no secure cognate in any related language: not in the other Germanic tongues, not in Latin, not anywhere. It is one of the genuine dead ends of English etymology — a curse-word whose own past is, fittingly, cursed to obscurity.

The obscurity of the word is in inverse proportion to the vividness of the thing. The curse is the best-attested magic in the entire archaeological record. From across the Greek and Roman world survive thousands of defixiones — thin lead tablets inscribed with binding curses, folded, pierced with a nail, and buried in graves or wells or springs. John Gager's collection catalogues them: curses against rival shopkeepers, against chariot teams, against lovers and thieves and litigants. This is not the rarefied magic of the philosopher-magus; it is the everyday hostility of ordinary people, made permanent in lead.

Magic: The Gathering makes 'Curse' an Aura subtype — an enchantment you fasten onto an opponent, so it sits on their side of the table, doing them slow harm. That is, remarkably, the exact logic of the defixio: a small inscribed object, aimed at a specific person, that binds misfortune to them at a distance. The oldest hostile magic we can dig up is still, on the cards, hostile magic — and the word for it still refuses to say where it came from.

sources.label: Etymonline, s.v. curse
John G. Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World
the corpus (Curse Aura subtype)

mage.title: Mage: from a Persian priesthood to a fantasy class
mage.dek: The root of the word 'magic' itself is a foreign fire-priest.
mage.html:
Mage is the lean, lofty synonym at the center of the whole family — and it is the family's root. It descends from Latin magus, from Greek magos, from Old Persian maguš: the Zoroastrian priestly caste of Persia, keepers of fire and readers of the stars. Their name is the origin not only of mage but of magic, magician, and the biblical magi. To use any of these words is to name a Persian priesthood you have probably never thought about.

The word's double edge was fixed early. To a Greek or a Roman the magos could be a revered eastern sage or a suspect foreign charlatan, and the New Testament sharpened the split: the wise men who followed the star are magi, but Simon 'the magus' in Acts is the false wonder-worker whose name gives us simony. The Renaissance then staged a deliberate rehabilitation. Ficino, Pico, and Agrippa reclaimed the magus as the philosopher-magician, the wise co-worker with nature — the figure Frances Yates made famous as the 'Renaissance Magus,' and whom Paola Zambelli's sources still gloss with the old commonplace that magus simply 'means wise man in Persian.'

Magic: The Gathering uses mage more than almost any other magic-word in its flavor text — some 417 appearances — and built an entire set, Strixhaven, around five colleges of them. It is a long way from a fire-altar in Persia to a card subtype, but the word has carried, intact, the same essential claim the whole time: that some people know how the cosmos really works, and can act on it.

sources.label: Etymonline, s.v. magus / mage (Greek magos ‹ Old Persian maguš)
Frances Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic
the corpus (mage flavor-text frequency; Strixhaven)

theurgy.title: Theurgy: the high magic that worked on the gods
theurgy.dek: Greek 'god-working' — the sacramental magic of Iamblichus and Proclus, and the alibi that made a thousand years of learned magic respectable.
theurgy.html:
Theurgy — Greek theourgia, 'god-working' (theos + -ergon) — was the highest magic the ancient world knew: not the commanding of spirits but the sacramental ritual by which the later Neoplatonists, Iamblichus and Proclus, sought to purify the soul and ascend toward union with the divine. The RenaissanceMagicDB glosses it as 'the system of ritual practices… through which the soul achieves communion with the divine,' and sets it at the opposite pole from goetia, the low magic of coercing demons.

What keeps theurgy a live word rather than a museum piece is that this high/low split became the central defense of learned magic for a thousand years. Claire Fanger's Invoking Angels and Sophie Page's Magic in the Cloister show medieval monks adopting the theurgic frame — we are not conjuring demons, we are working piously toward God — to make angel-magic defensible. Michael Bailey's The Sacred and the Sinister maps exactly where the authorities drew, and redrew, that line.

Magic: The Gathering has no theurgy keyword, and the corpus shows it as a rare, literary word — which is the point. The distinction between holy and unholy magic, so central to real history, mostly dissolves in a game where all five colors cast freely. Theurgy is the word for the boundary the game erased.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. theurgia, theurgic, nous, goetia
Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices, 13th–16th Centuries
Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
Michael D. Bailey et al., The Sacred and the Sinister
Etymonline, s.v. theurgy (‹ Greek theourgia)

goetia.title: Goetia: the sorcerer who howls
goetia.dek: The shadow twin of theurgy — low magic, demon-commanding, named for the howl of the charm.
goetia.html:
Goetia — Greek goēteia, from goēs, 'a sorcerer who howls charms' — is the shadow twin of theurgy. Where theurgy purifies the soul, goetia coerces spirits; the RenaissanceMagicDB places it 'at the lowest and most disreputable' rung of the magical ladder, 'the practice of summoning and commanding demons.' The howling root marks it, from the start, as ritual noise-magic — the opposite of philosophy.

Its monument survives in the corpus in Joseph Peterson's edition of the Lesser Key of Solomon, whose first book, the Ars Goetia, catalogues seventy-two demons with their seals and offices — the direct ancestor of every fantasy 'bestiary of the damned.' Richard Kieckhefer's Magic in the Middle Ages and Frank Klaassen's Transformations of Magic trace how such conjuring-books circulated as the definitionally illicit craft — the very thing 'necromancy' had come to mean.

Magic: The Gathering built the demons of the Ars Goetia into its infernal pacts long before the word became a subculture. But the etymology keeps the older, stranger sense alive: goetia is not sophistication. It is a person howling the right words at the dark.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. goetia, evocatio
Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), The Lesser Key of Solomon (Ars Goetia)
Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages (Canto Classics)
Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
Etymonline, s.v. goety (‹ Greek goēteia)

exorcism.title: Exorcism: casting out by a stronger oath
exorcism.dek: You don't expel a demon by force — you bind it out with a superior oath. The method is in the name.
exorcism.html:
Exorcism — Greek exorkismos, 'a binding-out by oath' (ex- + horkos, 'oath') — reveals its method in its name. You do not expel a demon by force; you bind it out with a stronger oath, invoking a higher authority. The RenaissanceMagicDB defines the Latin exorcismus as 'the ritual expulsion of demonic entities… through the invocation of divine authority.'

In practice, as Frank Klaassen's The Transformations of Magic and Michael Bailey's The Sacred and the Sinister show, exorcism sat uneasily on the border between sanctioned Church rite and illicit conjuring: the same words that cast a demon out could, in the wrong hands, call one up. The grimoires Joseph Peterson edits — the Grimorium Verum, the Book of Magic — freely mix the two. The exorcist and the conjurer were often reading from the same page.

The word's oath-logic binds it to conjure and abjure — magic as a courtroom of promises made and unmade. In Magic: The Gathering the drama survives as flavor; the deep structure — that authority is a kind of stronger swearing — is pure medieval law.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. exorcismus
Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
Michael D. Bailey et al., The Sacred and the Sinister
Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Grimorium Verum; Book of Magic
Etymonline, s.v. exorcism (‹ Greek exorkizein)

conjure.title: Conjure: every conjuration is a contract
conjure.dek: From Latin 'to swear together' — the same root as conspire and jury. To conjure a spirit was to bind it by oath.
conjure.html:
Conjure comes from Latin coniurare, 'to swear together' (con- + iurare) — the same root as conspire and jury. To conjure a spirit was to bind it by a shared oath: a legal contract with the supernatural. The medieval conjuring-texts the corpus preserves are, quite literally, books of such oaths.

Richard Kieckhefer's Magic in the Middle Ages and Frank Klaassen's studies of 'illicit learned magic' (The Transformations of Magic; Making Magic in Elizabethan England) show conjuration as the core craft of the clerical underworld — the necromantic manuals and the ars notoria catalogued by Klaassen and Claire Fanger, in which a trained cleric compels angels or demons with the correct formula. The Picatrix (Attrell & Porreca) supplies the astrological machinery; the ritual side is all binding and adjuration.

Magic: The Gathering's Alchemy format revived 'conjure' as a keyword action — to create a named card out of nothing — which is, charmingly, the etymology reversed: not binding a spirit by oath, but simply speaking a thing into being. The word has come full circle, from contract to pure creation.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic; Making Magic in Elizabethan England
Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels; Rewriting Magic
Dan Attrell & David Porreca (trans.), Picatrix
Etymonline, s.v. conjure (‹ Latin coniurare)

pentacle.title: Pentacle: a printed circuit of holy names
pentacle.dek: Not the five-pointed star (that's the pentagram) but the whole charged object — the magician's interface with the powers.
pentacle.html:
Pentacle — Medieval Latin pentaculum, on Greek pente, 'five' — is not, strictly, the five-pointed star (that is the pentagram); it is the whole charged object. The RenaissanceMagicDB defines pentaculum precisely: 'a disc or plate — of metal, parchment, or wax — inscribed with protective and operative symbols, divine names,' worn or wielded during ritual.

The pentacle is a working tool of the Solomonic tradition the corpus documents in detail: the pentacles of the Key of Solomon and Joseph Peterson's Grimorium Verum; the sigils and figures Frank Klaassen edits in Making Magic in Elizabethan England and analyzes in The Transformations of Magic; the elaborate diagrams of Skinner's Ars Notoria. Each pentacle is a compressed spell — divine names and characteres arranged to protect the magician or compel a spirit.

Modern usage flattened the pentacle into a spooky decoration, but its history is engineering: it is the interface between the magician and the powers, a printed circuit of holy names. Magic: The Gathering's talismans and seals are its distant, humming descendants.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. pentaculum
Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Grimorium Verum; Key of Solomon
Frank Klaassen, Making Magic in Elizabethan England; The Transformations of Magic
Stephen Skinner & Daniel Clark (eds.), Ars Notoria
Etymonline, s.v. pentacle (‹ med.L. pentaculum)

grimoire.title: Grimoire: the spell-book that was only ever a grammar
grimoire.dek: How a word for a Latin textbook became the name of Europe's forbidden books of magic.
grimoire.html:
The etymology is almost too neat to be true, and yet it is: grimoire is a French deformation of grammaire, 'grammar' — Greek grammatikē, 'the art of letters,' by way of Latin. In an age when literacy was rare and Latin was the shared tongue of the Church and of learned magic alike, a book of arcane learning and a book of grammar looked like the same dangerous thing. 'Grammar' grew a shadow-sense of occult knowledge, and the mispronounced grammar-book became the spell-book. (Its Scots cousin, along the very same path, became glamour.)

But a grimoire was never merely a metaphor; it was a real and specific kind of object, and Ted's medieval-magic library is full of them. Frank Klaassen, in The Transformations of Magic, distinguishes the two great streams of late-medieval learned magic — the image-and-astrology magic descending from the Arabic Picatrix, and the ritual 'necromancy' of conjuring texts — and shows how both were copied, hidden, and recopied in exactly these books. The RenaissanceMagicDB catalogues their contents as technical terms: the pentaculum, the exorcismus, the ligatura or binding, the characteres inscribed to compel a spirit.

The surviving grimoires named in the corpus read like the game's own flavor text made real: Joseph Peterson's editions of the Grimorium Verum and the Lesser Key of Solomon; the Ars Notoria, a grimoire that promised, like an MTG card, the instant infusion of knowledge; the conjuring manuals catalogued by Klaassen and by Sophie Page and Catherine Rider in The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. Even John Dee's angelic project, as Deborah Harkness shows in John Dee's Conversations with Angels, was in the end the making of a very learned grammar of the language of angels.

So the word remembers its own history exactly. A grimoire is a grammar — a book that teaches you to read a hidden language and, in reading it aloud, to act. The suspicion fossilised in the word is the whole medieval anxiety about who gets to hold a book.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic: Illicit Learned Magic in the Later Middle Ages and Renaissance
Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Grimorium Verum; The Lesser Key of Solomon
Sophie Page & Catherine Rider (eds.), The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
Deborah E. Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. pentaculum, exorcismus, ligatura, characteres
Etymonline / OED, s.v. grimoire (‹ grammaire ‹ grammar)

talisman.title: Talisman: the made object, and the art of drawing down a star
talisman.dek: From Greek 'consecration' through the Arabic Picatrix to Ficino's star-magic — the talisman is technology, not superstition.
talisman.html:
A talisman is, by the root, a finished thing. The word comes through Arabic ṭilsam from Byzantine Greek telesma, 'consecration, completion,' from telein, 'to complete, to consecrate.' That distinguishes it precisely from the amulet, which merely protects: a talisman is made powerful by ritual, deliberately charged, engineered. It is the closest the pre-modern world came to a manufactured magical device.

The engineering manual survives, and it sits in Ted's library. The Picatrix — Arabic Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm, 'the Goal of the Wise,' a 10th–11th-century compendium translated in Dan Attrell and David Porreca's edition — is essentially a talisman-maker's handbook: how to capture celestial spiritus in matter by choosing the right moment and the right material. The RenaissanceMagicDB preserves that technical vocabulary in Latin: the electio, the astrological election of the favourable hour; the lamina, the thin planetary metal-plate (gold for the Sun, silver for the Moon); the characteres inscribed to seize the power; the whole apparatus of dignitas and exaltatio that decides when a star is strong enough to draw down.

This is a real intellectual tradition with a pedigree, not folk superstition. Thābit ibn Qurra's De Imaginibus (in John Michael Greer's translation) gave the Latin West its theory of astral images; Marsilio Ficino, in the third book of his De vita, turned talisman-making into respectable Neoplatonic philosophy, capturing the spiritus mundi — the same Hermetic world-soul the RenaissanceMagicDB glosses under anima mundi. Frank Klaassen's Making Magic in Elizabethan England shows the practice alive and vernacular a century later, and John Dee's Sigillum Dei Aemeth — a great circular talisman built from angelic dictation — is its baroque masterpiece.

Magic: The Gathering knows the talisman mainly as a mana-rock — a made object that channels power — which is, remarkably, the exact ancient idea: a consecrated device for drawing energy from the cosmos into your hand. The card mechanic and the Picatrix agree on what a talisman is for.

sources.label: Dan Attrell & David Porreca (trans.), Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
John Michael Greer (trans.), De Imaginibus: Thābit ibn Qurra's Astral High Magic
Frank Klaassen, Making Magic in Elizabethan England
RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. electio, lamina, characteres, anima mundi, sigillum dei aemeth
Marsilio Ficino, De vita coelitus comparanda (via Copenhaver; Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition)
Etymonline / OED, s.v. talisman (‹ Arabic ṭilsam ‹ Greek telesma)

necromancer.title: Necromancer: how death-divination became the black arts
necromancer.dek: A single mistaken vowel turned a diviner who listens to the dead into a sorcerer who commands them.
necromancer.html:
A necromancer, to a Greek, was not someone who raised the dead to fight for them. The word is nekromanteia — nekros, 'corpse,' plus manteia, 'divination.' It named a specific ancient practice: summoning the spirits of the dead not as servants but as informants. Odysseus performs it in Odyssey XI, digging a pit of blood so the shades will speak. It belongs, properly, with divination — with scrying and augury — not with armies of the dead.

So how did the necromancer become the corpse-puppeteer of modern fantasy? Partly through a spelling accident, and Ted's databases document the drift. Medieval Latin, uneasy with the Greek, reshaped necromantia into nigromantia — as if from niger, 'black.' Death-divination was folk-etymologised into 'the black arts.' The RenaissanceMagicDB entry for necromantia preserves the original sense exactly — 'summoning or consulting the spirits of the dead to obtain hidden knowledge' — while Frank Klaassen's The Transformations of Magic and Claire Fanger's studies of ritual magic (Invoking Angels; Rewriting Magic) trace how the learned conjuring of spirits was recast, by hostile authorities, as the blackest of the illicit arts. Michael Bailey's The Sacred and the Sinister maps precisely that boundary-work.

Magic: The Gathering inherits the fantasy necromancer wholesale — the corpus overwhelmingly reanimates. But the older meaning is the better story, because it turns on a single mistaken letter. Change one vowel-cluster in a monk's Latin and you convert a diviner who listens to the dead into a sorcerer who commands them. Words, like the dead, don't always stay where you put them.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. necromantia
Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels; Rewriting Magic
Michael D. Bailey et al., The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic
Etymonline, s.v. necromancy (‹ Greek nekromanteia; med.L. nigromantia)

alchemy.title: Alchemy: the science with the Arabic article stuck on
alchemy.dek: Say it slowly and you can hear the seam: al-chemy, 'the-chemy,' the Arabic 'the' welded onto a Greek root.
alchemy.html:
Say alchemy slowly and you can hear the seam. That al- is the Arabic definite article, 'the' — the same one welded onto algebra, alkali, alcohol, algorithm. Each entered European languages through Arabic scholarship with its 'the' fused on so tightly that English speakers never noticed they were saying 'the-chemy.'

What follows is murkier. Arabic al-kīmiyāʾ takes up a Greek word, and scholars still argue which: perhaps khēmia, 'the Egyptian art,' from Khem, the 'black land' of the Nile; or khymeia, 'a pouring, an infusion.' Either way the word records a transmission — Greek natural philosophy preserved and transformed in the Arabic-speaking world, then handed back to a Europe that had forgotten it. The definitive account is in the corpus: Lawrence Principe's The Secrets of Alchemy and William Newman's work on the Latin Geber, alongside Lyndy Abraham's indispensable Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery, which glosses the whole symbolic vocabulary the game borrows.

That journey is the point, and it's fitting the word enacts it. Alchemy is the art of transmutation — of moving a substance across the boundary of its own nature — and its vocabulary is itself a great transmutation, Greek roots passed through Arabic gold and cooled into Latin. This study gives alchemy its own family: elixir and azoth (both Arabic), quintessence and vitriol (Latin), the athanor and the alembic. Trace them and you retrace how knowledge actually moved through the medieval world — never in a straight line, always with an article or two picked up along the way. (For the deeper history, cross-reference Ted's own alchemy databases and the Maier/Atalanta Fugiens materials.)

sources.label: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery (Cambridge)
Lawrence M. Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy; William R. Newman on the Latin Geber
Stanton J. Linden (ed.), The Alchemy Reader: From Hermes Trismegistus to Isaac Newton
TheosophicalAlchemyDB; Maier, Atalanta Fugiens materials
Etymonline, s.v. alchemy (‹ Arabic al-kīmiyāʾ)

phylactery.title: Phylactery: the amulet that fantasy turned into a soul-jar
phylactery.dek: Greek watch-charm, to Jewish prayer-box, to lich's soul-jar — one word, three owners.
phylactery.html:
Few words have travelled as far, or changed masters as completely, as phylactery. It begins in Greek as phylaktērion — literally 'a thing that guards' — from phylassein, 'to keep watch.' A phylactery was an amulet: a small protective object worn against harm, exactly the kind of charged charm that fills this study's classical-items family.

From there it acquired a religious home. In the Greek of the New Testament, phylaktēria names the tefillin — the small leather boxes of scripture bound to arm and forehead in prayer. Same logic as the amulet: sacred text, sealed in a container, worn on the body. For most of its English life, from the 14th century on, that is what a phylactery was — a scripture-case, or any protective charm.

Then a game rewrote it. In Dungeons & Dragons, a lich hides its soul inside a phylactery; destroy the object and you destroy the deathless wizard. The container-of-holy-power became a container-of-stolen-life — a total inversion of moral charge that keeps the core image perfectly intact: a small object in which something precious is sealed for safekeeping. Magic: The Gathering, notably, tends to keep closer to the older sense; the word remembers what it used to mean, even when we don't.

sources.label: Etymonline / OED, s.v. phylactery (‹ Greek phylaktērion)
Liddell–Scott–Jones, s.v. φυλακτήριον

scry.title: Scry: how a whisper-word became a keyword
scry.dek: A four-century-old crystal-gazing word acquired a brand-new technical meaning inside living memory.
scry.html:
Scry is a small miracle of English thrift — what's left when you file the front off descry, 'to catch sight of at a distance,' from Old French descrier, 'to cry out, announce.' The announcing faded and only the seeing remained; by the mid-1500s a scryer was a crystal-gazer, one who saw hidden or distant things in a reflective surface — the world of John Dee and his obsidian mirror.

For centuries it was a quiet, archaic word. Then Magic: The Gathering, in 2004, made Scry a keyword: look at the top N cards of your library, then reorder or bin them. A word that meant 'to see the hidden' became a mechanic for seeing — and gently rearranging — your own future. This study's Insights chart shows the result: 'scry' now lives overwhelmingly in rules text, a word that became a keyword inside living memory. Most words hide their history; scry wears a fresh coat of it.

sources.label: Etymonline / OED, s.v. scry, descry
Deborah E. Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels (on scrying practice)

glamour.title: Glamour & grimoire: two spells from one grammar
glamour.dek: A fashion word, a spell-book, and a dictionary — all the same word.
glamour.html:
Here is the best trick in the whole magical lexicon, and it is entirely true. Take the dry word grammar — Greek grammatikē, 'the art of letters.' In the Middle Ages, when nearly all serious learning was Latin and looked, to the unlettered, indistinguishable from sorcery, 'grammar' picked up a second life meaning occult learning. A book of that learning became a grimoire — French pronouncing 'grammar' badly, and the mispronunciation stuck to the spell-book. (See the full grimoire history.)

But there is a third child. In Scots, the same 'grammar' mutated into glamour — 'a magic spell, an enchantment that deceives the eye.' To 'cast the glamour' was to bewitch someone into seeing what wasn't there. Walter Scott put it into wide circulation in the early 1800s; from there it drifted, over a century, into 'dazzling, illusory attractiveness.' Call a film star glamorous and you call them, at the root, enchanted.

Grammar, grimoire, glamour: a dictionary, a spell-book, and a red-carpet adjective, all one syllable, all descended from the ancient suspicion that people who can read are dangerous. It's the thesis of this whole project — that the words for magic carry, fossilised inside them, whole attitudes about knowledge and power.

sources.label: Etymonline / OED, s.vv. glamour, grammar, grimoire
Walter Scott, Letters on Demonology and Witchcraft (popularises 'glamour')
